message.content:
Adversaries may use dynamic API resolution to obfuscate malware functionalities and hinder defensive analysis. This technique involves dynamically resolving API functions during runtime, thereby concealing malicious activities and avoiding static analysis detection. Malware typically uses OS-provided Native API functions for tasks involving processes, files, and system artifacts. Static artifacts, such as strings in payload files or import address tables (IAT), can reveal these functions to defensive analysts. By employing dynamic API resolution, adversaries can obscure these indicators until runtime, similar to software packing.

Common methods include storing hashes of function names instead of literal strings and using functions like GetProcAddress() and LoadLibrary() to manually link and load these functions during execution. These hashes can be further obfuscated using encryption or string manipulation techniques. Notable examples include Bazar, which hashes and resolves API calls at runtime, and the Lazarus Group, which uses custom hashing methods in shellcode.

Mitigation strategies involve employing advanced behavioral analysis and runtime monitoring to detect and analyze dynamically resolved API calls. Additionally, implementing robust deobfuscation techniques can help uncover concealed malware functionalities.
